comments: "TV on the radio here, sometimes on the telly too." This is a reference to Tommy's 'Top Of The Pops' appearances. He plays three tracks from a pre-release of the Phil Collins LP.

sessions.details: Details unknown. No known commercial release.
recorded 1974-10-31 (Peel Session #3), first TX (on Top Gear) 1974-11-14. No known commercial release.

track_listing.details: LP-The Wild, The Willing And The Innocent; Chrysalis

track_listing.track: 'The Roof Is Leaking (LP-Face Value)' (Virgin)thumb|Collins' solo début, rapturously receive by TV. Collins would not leave Genesis until 1995.
Many Are Called But Few Get Up

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. This is TV on the radio here, sometimes on the telly too.
Welcome to the Friday Rock Show.
Right, for your edification and delight, tonight we have a 1974 session by the group that had about 12 different lineups during their existence.
Those Welsh dragons called Man.
Also, we have a repeat of our recent set by Motörhead.
we've got classics by the floyd by the scorpions by elp and also by jefferson airplane we have
brand new stuff by rush phil collins and iron maiden also a good single by the tigers of pantang
not forgetting our friday night connection a new one and the answer to last week's as well
but this evening we start with this once when she can a little bit of pain deep in the night
That, of course, was the Pink Floyd from their album called The Wall,
a double album that came out well over a year ago,
and it's still nice to listen to.
The track was called Run Like Hell,
played especially for everybody at Alcan Rogerstone in Gwent in South Wales.
Best wishes from Pete Ward, who lives in Ritzka in Gwent.
If I can only find out where to buy tickets to go and see them in Germany,
I'll get myself some.
They're doing The Wall over there.
I think it's in Dortmund sometime this month.
Prior to that, you heard UFO,
and a track from The Wild, The Willing, and The Innocent,
which is their new album.
The track was called Lonely Heart.
That was for all the headbangers of Duffer's House in Gordonstone,
a rather famous school up there at the top of our country,
particularly Danny, Skull, and Arthur.
And also, Lonely Heart was most especially for Squire,
who wants me to shout that Squire loves heavy metal!
First of a few on tape by Motorhead.
I must say that I really still consider that album
to be really the best of the year.
Or what was it?
At least about a year ago when it came out.
Still a tough album to beat.
Animal Magnetism is the name of the album,
the name of the band, but the Scorpions,
the track we played was called Make It Real.
And I played it for a lady who at the moment is ill in bed,
I'm unhappy to say.
Her name is Sarah.
Best wishes from Mark the Unclean One.
That's how he signs his letter.
Also wants to say hello to Mick and to Wilma
and all the, I think it's,
pouring Landon Brook headbangers.
Also played that Scorpion's track for Helen and Jeff and Andrew Woods and Paul Taylor and Satsuma
Naughty little orange
And for Wolf and Andrew C and Rob and Lefty and everybody else who followed the Love Drive album
Off me to ogle the cover, it says
Julie Mason wrote this letter in Bullwell in Nottingham, huh, Julie?
Like also to dedicate that Scorpion's track to a lady who's got a birthday
I had a birthday on the February 7th
Actually, I can't remember the day
Is it the 2nd or what?
It's the 30th
It's the 30th
She's got a birthday coming up on the 2nd, which is a few days away.
That particular lady is a lady by the name of Sandra.
She is the wife of Barry Evans.
Sandra Evans lives at 35 DC Road, Hartford in Huntingdon, in Cambridgeshire.
Barry wants me to tell you that he loves you, but I think you know that anyway.
And he also loves the kids.
And he hopes that you're very well.
So, Barry, crawl over to the other side of the room and say hello to her.
Properly.
And before that, you heard something by Mudderhead.
It was called Live to Win.
And we've got more by Motorhead as the evening progresses.
It's 23 minutes exactly after 10.
Here's something from the new but not yet available album by Phil Collins.
From an album that's not coming out till February the 13th.
I think I'm accurate in saying that, so don't go looking for it tomorrow in your record stores,
because it is not out till about February 13th.
I know I'm ultra-enthusiastic about albums.
I remember vividly talking to Manfred Mann once, and he said to me,
the one thing about you, mate, is that you're really enthusiastic about music.
I find it hard to contain my enthusiasm with regard to Phil Collins' solo album.
I think it's an absolute masterpiece.
The album has the title Face Value.
The tracks that I've just played you are Hand in Hand, Droned, and The Roof is Leaking.
The album incorporates some of the finest musical talents in this world,
including that of Mr. Phil Collins.
On one track, there's Eric Clapton, then there's Alphonse, so Johnson playing the bass.
It's just an amazing album.
At least that's my opinion, and probably it's down to the fact that I've heard it 12 or 13 times.
It's magic. It's called face value.
25 minutes now to 11 of the clock.
Way back in 1974, the BBC recorded this particular session by man.
Here's one of about three tracks we're going to play you tonight
by that amazing Welsh ensemble.
This is Day and Night.
And that's just an extract from one side of an album,
the album that bore the title of Tarkus,
that came out way back in 1971 by ELP.
Played that specially to Gladden the Heart and the Ears of Paul Wade,
who lives at No. 25 Chestnut Road in Strewed in Kent.
And before that, you heard something from our archive session tonight by Mann,
put together way back in 1974.
The track we played was called Day and Might.
In Mann at that time, and they had 12 changes, it seems, during their whole history,
Mickey Jones on the guitar, he was really the constant of the band,
and Deke Leonard, Ken Whaley, and Mr. Terry Williams.
Who now, of course, plays drums for lovable Dave Edmonds.
And later tonight on the Friday Rock Show, we're going to play that classic by them called
Many Are Called, But Few Ever Bothered to Get Up.
A little trio, aren't they?
Motorhead and Fire Fire, which is the track you'll find on their album Mesa Spades.
Specially for Crawley, Headbangers, all of them, including Wes and Steve and Ian and Mark and Roger and Hans and Viv.
And Ryan and Chatfield, and myself, myself being James Miller, who lives in Broadfield in Crawley in Sussex.
And also for a lady by the name of Louise Bold, wherever she is, and I'm sure she is, from A Man Called Leek.
And before that, you heard something from the new album by Iron Maiden, the album's called Killers.
The track was Drifter, by Ivan Pickett of Sanford Hill, and all the Iron Maiden fans in Longton.
It's 5 to 11, it's time for the Friday Night Connection.
Now, the tracks we played you last week were these, Parallels from Going for a Song by Yes,
The Eleventh Earl of Mar by Genesis from Wind and Wuthering
and Lost Horizons by Michael Schenker
from the album called The Michael Schenker Group.
Lots of answers, only about 30 or so
who were absolutely accurate and correct.
The answer was this.
The connection is that all the album's sleeves
were designed by hypnosis,
which was staring you right in the face.
But it's not really an easy thing to work out
when you're listening to music,
thinking that you've got to look to album sleeves,
but that nonetheless was the answer.
that gets a £10 record token for Chris O'Hearn,
who's the 10 All Saints Road, King's Heath in Birmingham.
Well done, Chris. You spotted it.
We'll send you the £10 record token just as soon as we can find the money,
and you know in this day and age it's particularly difficult.
It wasn't too difficult, though, for you to put together a Friday Night Connection.
Three bits of music that have, inherent in them,
some sort of theme, or indeed a Friday Night Connection.
Friday Night Connection for Delight.

who lives at number 10, that's right, All Saints Road, King's Seat in Birmingham.

Wherever it is, it's definitely in there, and Chris knows where it is, if you think you know.
All you have to do is to jot it down on a postcard and send it to
The Friday Night Connection, The Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
We happen to pull your card out of the bag at the latter part of next week.
you'll get yourself a £10 record token.

Once again, the address is The Friday Night Connection,
The Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
And good luck.
It's 12 minutes after one, here's a correction.
The Yes track, which of course was called Parallels and we played for you last week,
was not in fact from the album called Going For A Song,
it was from the album called Going For The One.
Have you ever heard of premature senility?
well, there's a very strong chance you might be listening to an extremely good example of it at the moment.
My apologies.
So, as recompense, here's something you probably have never heard before,
unless you happened to be in the studio when they were recording it.
A couple of tracks from the brand new Rush album.
The album has the title, Moving Pictures.
I'm not absolutely sure when the album's coming out, but it isn't too long.
The group are called Rush, the album's called Moving Pictures, two tracks from it.
That track was Limelight, you just heard, and before that a track called Red Barchetta.
I'd like to dedicate that one to everybody, all the headbangers, that is, at the Sand and Comprehensive,
with very best wishes from Patrick Welsh, who lives at Red Gable.
Spring elms lame in Lane, rather, in Little Baddo, I think it is.

Nice hypnosis sort of postcard with it.
And for Mick Dyer, who's convalescing at the moment from an appendix operation.
Best wishes from Terry Geary, who lives in Enderby in Leicestershire.
Would you care for a really accurate time check?
Well, it is one, two, three, twenty-three and three-quarter minutes past eleven.
